summary: My mother, Margaret Alice Wilson, was born in the Sand Hills of western Oklahoma shortly after the 20th century. She grew up living in a half dugout on the great plains and was a very religious woman deeply involved in the Pentecostal Holiness Church. Despite her limited formal education, she was ordained in the church and was a strong influence in our family, especially after my father died.

I grew up in Oklahoma, specifically around Tulsa and the western parts like Red Fork. My family lived through the Dust Bowl era, and my father worked for the Frisco railroad in Tulsa. Oklahoma shaped much of my early life, from my schooling at Daniel Webster High School to my involvement in local organizations and newspapers.

My father, Dwight Henry Carter, was born in Lane, Kansas, and grew up partly in Syracuse, Kansas. He was a cowboy and wrangler in his youth, riding horses and herding cattle. After the Dust Bowl, he found work with the Frisco railroad in Tulsa but tragically died in a workplace accident in December 1949. His cowboy rifle even passed down to my great-grandson, who loves to ride sheep and pretend to be a cowboy.

Lane, Kansas, is where my father, Dwight Henry Carter, was born. Although his family later moved around western Kansas, his mother returned to Lane because she disliked the Great Plains. My father was raised by his father in Lane before spending time in Syracuse and eventually moving west.

Syracuse, Kansas, was part of my father's early life, though there were few schools there. He spent some of his youth in that area, learning a bit of education before becoming a cowboy. It was a tough place with limited opportunities, which shaped his rugged lifestyle.

After the Dust Bowl, my father was fortunate to get a job with the Frisco railroad in Tulsa as a laborer. He painted box cars and tank cars, but tragically, in December 1949, a paint pot exploded and he was fatally burned. The Frisco railroad was a significant part of his later life and livelihood.

I graduated from Daniel Webster High School in Tulsa, where I was involved in school activities and even contributed to the school newspaper. During my senior year, I also served as president of the Tulsa Inner School Council, which aimed to reduce violence between schools like Webster and Rogers Central. Webster High was an important part of my youth and education.

I was president of the Tulsa Inner School Council during my senior year at Webster High School. The council was an effort between schools like Rogers Central and Webster to make peace after violent incidents, especially following football games. It was a response to the rowdy times in Oklahoma schools back then.

Rogers Central High School was one of the schools involved in the Tulsa Inner School Council, which I led as president during my senior year at Webster. There was a lot of rivalry and even violence between Rogers Central and Webster, so the council was formed to ease tensions. It was a significant part of the school community in Tulsa at that time.

My first newspaper job was at the West Tulsa Paper in the summer of 1950, right after I graduated from Webster High School. I worked for Kyle Tinny, who was a comedian and journalist and had bought the paper. I earned $20 a week and that experience helped launch my career in journalism.

After my father found work post-Dust Bowl, we lived on a small acreage in Red Fork, Oklahoma. We kept farm animals like cows, horses, pigs, and raised our own chickens and milk. It was a subsistence-type farm that gave me a pioneer-like upbringing on the prairie.

I attended the University of Tulsa, earning degrees in economics and later journalism in 1957 and 1959. When I started, TU was mostly a technology school aimed at preparing students for manual labor jobs, and I struggled initially without a math or foreign language background. The GI Bill helped finance my education after my active duty in the army.

I was about seven years old when Pearl Harbor was bombed on December 7, 1941. It was a traumatic time with fears of saboteurs poisoning water and causing harm. I remember being very alert to dangers, even mistaking a sewer cover for something suspicious. That event marked the beginning of World War II for us.

When I was young, one of my older brothers had a newspaper route for the Tulsa World. He paid me a small amount to help deliver papers, which got me reading newspapers from an early age. That early exposure helped foster my lifelong love of journalism.

To this day, I read the Norman Transcript regularly along with other newspapers. It’s part of my daily routine to stay informed and connected to Oklahoma news. Reading newspapers has been a lifelong habit since my youth.

The Oklahoman is one of the newspapers I subscribe to and read regularly. It keeps me connected to state news and events, and I appreciate holding the print edition in my hands. Newspapers have always been a big part of my life.

I also subscribe to and read the print edition of The New York Times every day. Even though it’s a national paper, I find it important to stay informed on broader issues. Holding the physical newspaper is something I prefer over digital versions.

The GI Bill of Rights was crucial in financing my college education after my two years of active duty in the army. It provided me with $110 a month, which helped cover tuition and living expenses. Without it, completing my degrees at the University of Tulsa would have been much harder.

My mother was deeply involved in the Pentecostal Holiness Church and was eventually ordained there. She was very religious and committed to that faith, which influenced our family life. The church’s worship style was quite distinctive and important to her identity.

I am a Methodist and have stayed committed to that faith throughout my life. While my mother was Pentecostal Holiness, I found my spiritual guidance through Methodism and looking to Jesus. It’s a faith that grounds me personally.
For spiritual guidance, I often look to Jesus. He is my religious leader and source of comfort. While I also appreciate other figures for different aspects of life, Jesus remains central to my faith.

Will Rogers is a leader I look up to for good sense in politics and civic affairs. Alongside Jesus, he has been a guiding figure in my life. I have also been involved with the Will Rogers Memorial Commission, which connects me further to his legacy.

I served as director of the Will Rogers Memorial Commission, a role that connected me to the legacy of Will Rogers. My wife Michelle followed me in that position, continuing the work. The commission is important in preserving Rogers’ contributions to Oklahoma and the nation.

My wife Michelle was Vice President of Fidelity Bank before taking on roles in public relations and the Will Rogers Memorial Commission. Her career at Fidelity Bank was part of her distinguished professional background. It reflects her leadership and expertise in the financial sector.

Michelle also served as Director of Public Relations for the Oklahoma Heritage Association. I met her through connections involving the association and Harold Stuart, who was president of the organization. The association plays a role in preserving Oklahoma’s history and culture.

Harold Stuart was a man of great integrity and civic-mindedness who connected me with my wife Michelle. He was president of the Oklahoma Heritage Association and Vice President of Cameron University in Lawton. I admired his character and had many encounters with him in Oklahoma and Washington.

Harold Stuart was Vice President of Cameron University in Lawton, Oklahoma, when I met him. The university is part of the broader Oklahoma educational landscape. Stuart’s role there was one of his many leadership positions.

Lawton, Oklahoma, is where Cameron University is located, and it was part of Harold Stuart’s professional life as Vice President. I have connections to Lawton through Stuart and his work in the state. It’s one of the many Oklahoma communities I’m familiar with.

I attended Harold Stuart’s wedding in Florida, which was a memorable event with notable guests like Francis Langford singing. It was a special occasion that I recall fondly. Florida was the setting for that important personal milestone.

At Harold Stuart’s wedding in Florida, we heard Francis Langford sing 'I'm In the Mood for Love.' She was a wonderful songstress and added a special touch to the ceremony. It was a memorable moment from that day.

Harold Stuart adopted two children from Norway, John Stuart and his sister, who were very fortunate to be part of his family. I have visited Norway and Sweden and sometimes mix them up, though I’m aware of their distinct cultures. Norway figures into the story of the Stuart family’s generosity.

Sweden is often mentioned alongside Norway, and I’ve visited both countries. The children Harold Stuart adopted might have otherwise had difficult lives in Sweden or Norway. I sometimes confuse the two, but they are distinct places in Scandinavia.

I never served in Korea during the Korean War but was involved in shipping troops to Korea from North Port Louis. It was a secretive operation to protect troop movements from enemy attacks. I served two years active duty and then in the reserves but was never called up for combat.

During my army service, I helped ship troops to Korea by sending them to Tacoma, Washington, where they boarded troop carriers in secret nighttime operations. Tacoma was a key embarkation point during that period. It was part of the sensitive logistics of the Korean War effort.
